data.title: On Holding: Valuation At Low Levels While Growth Doesn't Stop
data.description:
Investment thesis

On Holding (NYSE:ONON) continues to report growth thanks to strong international expansion, diversification in its product portfolio and a greater presence in the direct-to-consumer channel, which would be a foundation for growth in the coming years. However, the stock is at a historically low valuation and has fallen more than 30% since its peak in early 2025, which presents a buying opportunity.

International growth kicks in

Note: This is an update to my previous article where I covered the ONON business model in more detail.

A little over a year ago I published an analysis of On Holding, which at the time was beginning to establish itself as a relevant player in the premium running segment. My thesis back then was clear: although ON was small compared to giants like Nike or Adidas, its accelerated growth and ability to maintain high gross margins made it an interesting story.

Today, many things have changed, but what hasn't is the brand's traction. If we look at Google Trends as a popularity thermometer, we see that interest in ON continues to grow globally, with the United States as the main driver, but also with clear signs of expansion in Europe, Asia and the Middle East. This confirms that the brand is gaining cultural relevance, which doesn't surprise me given the fact that you only have to look at people's shoes in your daily life to realize that ON is everywhere. And if you ask people about the brand, they'll usually tell you it was worth the purchase.

This geographic expansion is also reflected in the company's financials. Sales in the Americas remained strong in the first half of 2025, with a 24% growth. However, Asia-Pacific, which includes key countries such as China, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan or Australia, grew by an extraordinary 115% year-over-year. This represents an acceleration of international growth, with this region growing only by 84% in 2024 and 76% in 2023. And the Europe, Middle East and Africa region also contributed with growth of 38.5% in the first half of 2025 compared to 18% in 2024.

The importance of this international growth lies in diversification. For a brand like ON, relying too heavily on the U.S. market could limit its long-term potential, so expanding across regions not only reduces geographic risk but also increases brand resilience, as consumer demand in one market can offset weakness in another. More importantly, success in Asia-Pacific and EMEA shows that ON's premium positioning works globally, not just in its home markets. This means that accelerated growth can be maintained for more years if things go well.

Direct-to-Consumer channel is key

Another important aspect of ON’s evolution is its retail strategy. The company continues to expand its network of flagship stores in cities like Paris, New York and Hong Kong, while also pushing its digital channels. Direct-to-consumer (or DTC) sales already represent more than 41% of total revenue in the first half of 2025 compared to 40.7% in 2024 and 37.5% in 2023, outpacing wholesale growth with 47% sales growth in the first half of 2025 compared to 23% for the wholesale channel.

This is relevant because DTC not only improves margins by cutting out intermediaries, but also strengthens the relationship with customers through controlled brand experiences. It wasn't for nothing that Nike tried to aggressively boost its DTC channel in 2021 to rely on its own stores by cutting relationships with retailers like Amazon, Macy's, Foot Locker or Urban Outfitters, but in its case it pulled back after realizing it couldn't fully replace wholesale partners. ON, on the other hand, seems to be striking a better balance, combining wholesale reach with premium flagships that serve as brand billboards (similar to what we see in luxury groups like Hermès or LVMH) where stores are less about volume and more about showcasing identity and exclusivity, at the same time that it continues to grow in the wholesale channel taking advantage of its relationships with retailers

Another element that reinforces the relevance of the DTC strategy is the company's progress in apparel and accessories. While shoes remain the backbone of the business with 94% of sales, this amount has been reducing very little by little and apparel sales grew from just $47 million in 2022 to more than $100 million in 2024 and are up another 67.5% in the first half of 2025. Accessories, although still very small, doubled in 2025 after a 50% growth in 2024.

These categories increase the possibility of cross-selling and full brand immersion for customers. In other words, every flagship store and every click on the ON app is no longer just an opportunity to sell sneakers, also it's a chance to dress customers head-to-toe, which if we see the margins of brands like Lululemon we can realize that it is something very profitable.

Valuation

After a strong start to the year, the company says it expects to end the year with at least CHF 2.91 billion in sales, which would represent a growth of almost 30%. This is very conservative considering that the first half of the year saw a 37.5% growth and the second half is usually the best, as it includes Thanksgiving, Black Friday and Christmas shopping.

So for this year, I think 35% growth or more is possible and then I estimate that the average annual growth for the coming years would be 25%. If we add a gradual expansion of margins as the company stops investing so much in SG&A expenses and improves its operational efficiency, then it could be earning just over CHF 1 billion in net profit in five years. The industry average is a P/E of 20 times, but as a company with untapped growth, above-average margins and a premium brand, I suppose 25 times would be logical too.

So a market cap of $26 billion divided by the current 332 million shares oustanding represents a price per share of $80 or an upside of just under 90% from current price.

While it's not the biggest bargain out there, the fact that valuation multiples are at rock-bottom levels strikes me as an opportunity right now. But, as I said, it's not the cheapest company you can find.

Risks

One of the main risks for ON is that its products belong to the consumer discretionary sector, and recently even premium brands are being affected by the fact that they can easily be postponed in an adverse economic environment. Ultimately, that "these new sneakers can wait" mentality has reached us all when our pocket is a bit tight. And the most recent reports from the retail sector by Jones Lang LaSalle reflect warning signs, such as the fact that 2024 saw the highest level of U.S. bankruptcy filings since 2010 and retail net absorption in 2025 showed 14.5 million square feet in the first half of the year, that is, many more stores are closing than opening.

And despite increased retail spending, consumer sentiment fell in Q1 2025 to its lowest level since 2022, reflecting economic and political uncertainty, which could end up impacting ON sales in the short-term.

Competition also represents a structural risk for ON. The sportswear market is dominated by giants like Nike, Adidas or Puma, who have been top of mind for decades among consumers looking for activewear. This means that ON is fighting for consumer attention, shelf space and digital visibility in an increasingly crowded market. The risk is that if ON's image begins to become outdated or consumers begin to notice that its prices are already too high, the company's differentiation could narrow quickly and the loss of customers could be abrupt, leading to cases like the recent declines of Lululemon and Nike.

Being a premium retail brand is fun, until it stops being one, sales stop growing, margins shrink and your stock plummets.

The bottom line

While the retail situation is currently delicate, ON has demonstrated continued growth and diversification across geographies, sales channels and products, which adds resilience to the business and allows for high-growth rates for many years to come.

Considering the valuation is at rock-bottom levels and although I don't expect it to be one of the top-performing stocks in the coming years, I do think there's value in the current price, especially for a brand that has demonstrated a strong value proposition for the consumer, which is why it seems like a good entry point with a 5 to 10 year time horizon.

data.title: Rivian: The New Georgia Plant Is A Game Changer
data.description:
Updating My Bullish Take On Rivian

My coverage history of Rivian Automotive (NASDAQ:RIVN) stock has seen both bearish and bullish ratings, with the most recent article, which came out in March 2025, suggesting a likely reversal in RIVN stock in the medium term. And it seems to be playing out almost the way I expected back then, with RIVN gaining ~8.58%, although it's far from the S&P 500 index's (SPY) gain of over 16% for the same time.

Now I see that my bullish thesis has only strengthened 2 days ago, when Rivian announced the addition of their new manufacturing facility in Georgia that "will eventually produce the midsize, 5-seater R2 SUV, and R3 crossover", according to the statement. I think that the official groundbreaking should help Rivian stock offset the macro uncertainties in the niche and give it a valuation boost throughout 2H 2025, and probably further into the next year. The narrative should finally get a boost because the new plant only increases Rivian's chances for further market share gains in the US, and it spells "more interested stock buyers" to me. This is why I decided to leave my "Buy" rating on RIVN unchanged for now.

Why Do I Think So?

Looking at Rivian's Q2 results, we see that it looks pretty "ugly" on the surface, because the firm missed on the bottom line by over 25%, according to Seeking Alpha, and their positive beat on the top line was less than 1%, so it looked insufficient to offset the former headline figure's pessimism. But in reality, the stock didn't fall that much after Q2 came out:

Moreover, Rivian even surged by over 13% since August 4th (the day before they released earnings), so the market seemingly didn't view the Q2 EPS miss as something tragic.

If we think about the reasons for the Q2 miss, we'll find that the main thing that drove this was the drop in production: Rivian delivered only 5,979 vehicles for the quarter, which was almost half of the 10,661 vehicles in Q2 2024. Why? The management said on the Q2 earnings call that the real cause for this wasn't the demand slump as it might seem at first glance - it was all about the supply chain-related issues and only partially shifts in trade policy.

We saw a significant decrease in production volume compared to the first quarter as a result of a variety of supply chain-related complexities partially driven by shifts in trade policy.

So, with the operating leverage in place, which tends to work in both directions, Rivian's bottom line was hit the hardest, and that's why the management had no choice but to lower the FY2025 EBITDA loss projections to the new $2-2.25 billion range.

But it doesn't address the main question, "Why didn't the stock dip further since this ugly Q2 print?"

The market is (almost) always a forward-looking mechanism that can price any unprofitable entity well above its high-ROE peer, solely based on future projections. So, in this particular case, Wall Street didn't rely on Rivian's Q2 as if it were a litmus paper. Instead, it decided to focus on what can bring in a massive advantage over other EV peers in the niche, and it is definitely their Software business, which showed ~$376 million in revenue for Q2 at a healthy $129 million gross profit. Nearly half of that revenue came from Rivian's JV with Volkswagen, and it's likely to be just the beginning of real monetization going forward because I think more brands will be looking for joint venture opportunities with Rivian as the latter scales further.

Another thing that we heard on the Q2 call was the updates on Rivian's upcoming R2 platform. We saw obviously that R1 was a groundbreaking product, which gained a lot of buyers' attention, but it was just too complex in manufacturing/assembling, so the firm couldn't quickly scale production and make a profit out of it. Now, with the new R2, which was initially designed to be more cost-efficient and easier to build, Rivian plans to launch the R2 from the existing Illinois facility, and I think it's going to benefit from the shared fixed-cost absorption.

The production volume output of Q2 going from in Q1, around 14,000 units to Q2 being around 6,000 units. The lack of fixed cost absorption, you can really see it in the numbers. And as we said, that was reflective of a lot of the supply chain environment that we're in and some of the trade-related and export control related items that we encountered in Q2, but an important point to call out here is that as we launch R2, the benefits of fixed cost absorption, which will be felt not just by R2, but carrying that across R1, we'll also start to see. And this is really important for us as we grow volume in the plant in our Normal, Illinois production facility going into 2026.

But Q2 data and commentary have been known to the market for over a month now, and it's not the main pillar of my thesis update to date.

As I noted in the very beginning of this article, about 2 days ago, Rivian announced the official groundbreaking of its new manufacturing plant in Georgia, which is going to "produce up to 400,000 vehicles annually by 2028 and employ 7,500 people" (once it's complete, of course).

I call it a game-changer for Rivian's growth story because it helps them shift from a niche EV SUV maker to a mass-market one. If we think about it for a while, their Illinois plant, even at the full capacity of 215,000 units, was never supposed to be enough to satisfy the growing SUV demand in the EV space. As legacy automakers such as General Motors (GM) and Ford (F) are now stepping back on their EV businesses, Rivian is clearly doubling down (which is obvious, because RIVN has the needed expertise). And now, with the new plant in Georgia, Rivian is going to have better scalability prospects over the next few years, and I believe it's exactly what's needed for a narrative change on the forward-looking Street.

The immediate effect on the news was bleak, I agree, but we already see the EPS positive revisions since the Q2 release date, and I think that the Georgian plant can further accelerate this once new updates from analysts covering the stock start to come out.

The stock price is set to reprice higher as a result of this narrative change, in theory, and its sales-based valuation on a forward basis lags Tesla's (TSLA) ratio by a factor of 5 or so:

I know that I'm comparing apples to oranges in a way when I try to match Tesla and Rivian, but for both of them, the EV business remains a core one. And unlike Tesla, which already had its EV glory days, Rivian's story is only getting started:

Over the next 5 years, Rivian's sales are projected to grow with a CAGR of 47.85%, while Tesla's sales are going to expand by only 26% annually for the same period. So, Rivian's P/S of 2.4x looks way more tempting for growth investors, in my view, and the premium versus Chinese EV brands that exist looks reasonable to me as well.

All Rivian needs to keep reversing higher is a confirmation of the narrative shift, which I expect to come throughout 2H 2025 with more upward-sloping earnings revisions. That's why I remain bullish on RIVN, at least for the medium term.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

The main risk to my thesis is that Rivian is going to stay with a net loss for the next few years, and we saw in the past that the break-even consensus projections tend to get lower. So, there's a risk that what we see today in Rivian's consensus EPS estimates is off by a mile, and the firm will stay with deep losses for longer than currently expected.

Rivian has over $7 billion on its balance sheet, and this fact makes the firm quite well-financed for its expansion plans. But there's a risk of further dilution ahead, and if that dilution happens to be larger than bulls may expect, the current shareholders' long positions are going to take a hit.

Also, I may be plain wrong regarding RIVN's valuation. I argued that the price investors pay for Rivian today is reasonable, but I compared its multiples with Tesla's. The comparison sample is just too narrow. And if we compare RIVN with LI and Xpeng (XPEV), we'll see it overvalued.

Final Words

Despite the risk factors, I still believe that I did the right thing when I decided to upgrade Rivian stock to "Buy" in March 2025. Rivian's underperformance relative to the broader market doesn't look dramatic, and while the firm missed on its bottom line for Q2, the loss per share improved by a factor of 2 since 2022, so the trend is generally positive. Anyway, with the latest news around Rivian's Georgian plant, I believe the market's narrative regarding the firm's future should shift eventually with more positive earnings revisions ahead. With that in mind, I expect the stock's price to adjust higher, and it makes my stay bullish on RIVN for now.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: I Lost Up To 90%: My Worst REIT Investments
data.description:
I have been actively investing in REITs (VNQ) for well over a decade, and I have lost a lot of money along the way.

Anyone who pretends to only pick winners is either a liar or has never taken chances.

Investing is a constant learning journey, and some of the lessons have to be learned the hard way.

In today’s article, I am going to give you the chance to learn from my costly mistakes so that you can avoid the same costly losses and headaches.

CBL & Associates Properties, Inc. (CBL)

This is my biggest REIT investing failure ever. I lost about 90% of my capital, and the worst part is that this mistake could have been easily avoided.

In short, CBL is a mall REIT that traded at just around 4x FFO and an estimated 50% discount to its net asset value back when I invested in it nearly 10 years ago. It was also offering a 10%+ dividend yield that appeared well-covered with a low 50% payout ratio.

Back then, I thought that it was so cheap because the market had overreacted to the narrative that e-commerce would kill all malls. We, of course, agreed that this was a headwind, but we thought that this would primarily impact lower-quality malls, leaving the Class B and A segments mostly intact. CBL’s malls weren’t exceptional, but they were often the dominant properties in their secondary markets, and we thought that they would be fine long-term, especially as they add more services, entertainment, and other non-retail uses to their properties.

We were correct about that. Today, its malls are generating higher sales per square foot than ever.

But where we messed up our analysis was the balance sheet. Looking at the surface figures like LTV and Debt-to-EBITDA, it seemed that the company was fine.

But those metrics failed to take the growing capex needs into account. Suddenly, the company had to use a growing chunk of its cash flow to reinvest in its properties just to keep them competitive. Therefore, its actual cash flow available to service the debt and pay the dividend was a lot lower than we thought.

This first led to a dividend cut, and later, as the world shut down due to the pandemic, the company was pushed into bankruptcy.

I lost almost everything. Not all of it because I had switched to the preferred equity, which received a piece of the new common equity after it emerged from bankruptcy.

The lesson here is not to overlook capex and how it can radically change over time.

I think that it is a particularly important lesson for office REITs today. They seem cheap and conservatively leveraged if you look at surface figures. But if the capex surges in the coming years due to the severe oversupply, then the cash flow will get drained, and balance sheets will quickly become stretched.

This recently prompted BXP (BXP) to cut its dividend, despite having one of the strongest balance sheets in the sector. I think that this is a clear red flag of more pain to come.

Medical Properties Trust, Inc. (MPW)

I still own a small position in MPW, so I have not lost all hope, but I have been wrong so far and may be wrong again.

When I first invested in the company, it had an exceptional track record, having massively outperformed the broader market (SPY) up until then:

It had a unique investment strategy, buying hospitals with strong triple net leases, including CPI-based rent escalations, and earning large spreads over its cost of capital. Its properties also enjoyed high rent coverage ratios, which seemed to suggest that its tenants were profitable and unlikely to default on their leases. The management also had a good track record at handling occasional problem tenants, managing to release the properties to other operators.

It then became the target of short sellers who claimed that MPW’s biggest tenant was a lot weaker than what MPW was portraying.

My mistake here was to quickly dismiss the short sellers. Back then, my investment in MPW had been very successful, earning me very large returns. MPW’s management also had a great track record; they had significant equity invested in the company, and they assured us that their tenants were fine, pointing to their solid rent coverage ratios. Finally, I had also recently experienced a short attack targeting another REIT called Farmland Partners (FPI), which was proven to have been criminal in court. All of this led me to dismiss the short sellers, which was a big mistake in hindsight, as they proved to be correct.

Later, its biggest tenant, Steward, ended up filing for bankruptcy, which, coupled with the surge in interest rates, caused its share price to crash. The company became overleveraged with a high risk of an eventual default:

To be fair to MPW’s management, the pandemic black swan, of course, didn’t help either. It caused great pain to hospitals as it delayed a lot of elective surgeries and treatments, and significantly increased healthcare labor costs.

The lesson here is to take short sellers seriously. They are often highly sophisticated investors, and if they are taking the risk of betting against a particular company, it typically means that they have done very deep research.

MPW is now a very different story, as it has turned into a deep value recovery play that depends on future rate cuts and its ability to release properties. Some short sellers have closed their positions. Others continue to expect further downside.

Invesque Inc. (IVQ.U:CA)

Invesque is not officially structured as a REIT, but it is a listed landlord and essentially functions as if it were one.

Back when I first invested in it, the thesis was simple. It was trading at a far lower valuation and higher yield than its senior housing REIT peers Welltower (WELL) and Ventas (VTR). The lower valuation was due to it being primarily listed in Canada and not being structured as a REIT. The management had a great track record, having sold their previous REIT for large gains.

The downside was that it was more heavily leveraged with a 55% LTV, which increased risks. We thought that this was still fine and that the company would reach a higher valuation as it gradually improved its balance sheet.

But instead, the pandemic came shortly after, which caused great pain to senior housing communities.

It caused Invesque to become overleveraged and forced it to start selling assets to pay off debt, greatly reducing its cash flow and putting it at high risk of bankruptcy.

The lesson here is that nothing else matters if the balance sheet isn’t strong enough for the company to survive a black swan. There is no point in earning good returns for a few years if you then lose it all as the company becomes overleveraged and is forced to aggressively sell assets just to survive.

Closing Note

The common theme of my investing mistakes in the REIT sector is excessive leverage. Most of these REITs could have been fine, even despite their setbacks, had they had a strong balance sheet to absorb the shocks.

But their managers were too greedy, took on too much leverage, and paid the price.

As a shareholder, I was too greedy, seeking high yields at the expense of significant risk.

Lesson learned.

At High Yield Landlord, we generally stay away from such speculative deep value plays these days and focus primarily on higher-quality REITs facing temporary issues instead. Those have been our most successful investments over time.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Precious Metals Stocks Are Back With A Vengeance In 2025
data.description:
By Amee Shah Upadhyaya CFA, CA, Senior Analyst - Index Product Support

Precious metals stocks have roared back in 2025 after years of underperformance, fueled by geopolitical tensions and economic uncertainty. The FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index is up 86% YTD, massively outperforming global equities. Investors are turning to this asset class for its hedging power, diversification benefits, and resilience against inflation.

After two straight years of underperformance, precious metals-related stocks are back with a vengeance so far in 2025, driven by rising geopolitical tensions and tariff uncertainties.

As at the end of August 2025, the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index was up 86% in the year to date, outperforming our flagship global equity benchmark, the FTSE Global All Cap Index, by over 70%.

Performance - Total Return (USD)

This performance reflects rapidly rising interest in precious metals-related stocks such as gold miners. Market participants are increasingly turning to this asset class to serve as a hedge against volatility, improve diversification or increase a portfolio’s resilience to inflation.

What’s in the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining index?

Here are the key characteristics of the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining index, with data as at end-August 2025.

  • The index focuses on exposure to global players in the precious metals and mining industry
  • By precious metals, gold mining dominates the index (with a 91.8% weighting), while exposure to platinum, other metals, diamonds and gemstones takes up the remainder of the index
  • The index comprises 69 stocks, with a top 10 holdings concentration of 65%; however, as of the September 22, 2025 index review, the top 10 weights will come down to about 62% due to the inclusion of additional Canadian constituents, bringing the index constituent total to 92.
  • By country, Canada accounts for nearly half of the index weight (48.7%), followed by the US (17%), South Africa (15%) and Australia (9%).

Country Breakdown

Performance rebounded in 2025

The FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining index underperformed its reference benchmark (the FTSE Global All Cap index) in 2023 and 2024, despite healthy gains from the gold price. In the year to end-August 2025, the performance of the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining index has rebounded strongly, with a total return in excess of 70% ahead of the reference benchmark.

Calendar Year Total Returns USD (%)
Index YTD 2025 2024 2023 2022 2021 2020 2019 2018 2017 2016
FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index 85.72 8.25 3.31 -12.23 -5.65 27.22 48.13 -9.6 10.57 58.2
FTSE Global All Cap Index 14.75 16.83 22.19 -17.87 18.54 16.76 27.09 -9.62 24.38 8.98

Source: FTSE Russell. Data as of August 29, 2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future returns. Please see the end for important legal disclosures.

Annualised Total Return (% USD)

As at end-August, the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index had delivered long-term outperformance against the global equity market, delivering a 10-year annualized return of 17.4%, compared to 11.4% for the broader index.

This performance comes with significant tracking difference: the performance of the FTSE Global All Cap Precious Metals and Mining Index diverged from that of the FTSE Global All Cap Index by more than 10% (in absolute terms) in six of the nine years between 2016 and 2024.

This, of course, suggests that the index could deliver significant diversification benefits in a broader equity portfolio.

Precious metals and mining ETF landscape

As of August 2025, 16 US-domiciled precious metals ETFs held $35 billion in assets. After experiencing outflows of $2.3 billion in the first half of 2025, precious metals ETFs have seen inflows of $1.4 billion in the two months ended August 2025.[Note 1]

Time to revisit precious metals?

The dramatic recent rise in precious metals-related stock prices highlights the important role this asset class can play during periods of geopolitical and economic uncertainty.

The hedging qualities, diversification benefits and inflation resilience of these stocks may appeal to a broad range of market participants, including those seeking focused exposure to precious metals and mining companies and those looking at an asset allocation switch within a broader equity portfolio.

data.title: Pzena International Value Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Non-U.S. equity markets posted strong gains in the second quarter, helped by a weaker U.S. dollar, despite headwinds from rising U.S. tariffs and ongoing geopolitical volatility. Growth stocks outpaced value, driven by renewed momentum in the technology sector, and our portfolio underperformed its benchmark.

By sector, only energy detracted from absolute performance, and Alibaba (BABA) saw a partial reversal of its strong Q1 performance, as investors reassessed the implications of rising AI investment and continued macroeconomic weakness in China. Sanofi (SNY) declined after reporting mixed results from its late-stage drug pipeline, and lower global energy prices weighed on Equinor’s (EQNR) share price.

The financial sector was the standout performer, and the top individual contributor, U.K. grocer J Sainsbury (OTCQX:JSNSF), rebounded, as the company continued to execute well amid heightened competitive pressure in the U.K. grocery market. German auto supplier Continental (OTCPK:CTTAY)(OTCPK:CTTAF) benefited from investor optimism around its ongoing corporate restructuring and stronger-than-expected earnings, while Daimler Truck’s (OTCPK:DTRUY) valuation recovered, as concerns over tariff impacts and macroeconomic headwinds subsided throughout the quarter.

In the second quarter, we established new positions in two leading Japanese companies, capitalizing on attractive entry points. Murata (OTCPK:MRAAY) is a global leader in electronic passive components, particularly capacitors and inductors. Concerns related to currency fluctuations and China resulted in an attractive buying opportunity for a business we believe has sustainable cost and technology leadership, with long-term growth potential in EVs and AI-driven data centers. Daikin (OTCPK:DKILY)(OTCPK:DKILF), a global leader in air conditioning systems, is facing macroeconomic uncertainty in several geographies as well as tariff pressures. We believe there is upside potential through margin improvement and a renewed focus on capital efficiency. We added to our positions in Olympus (OTCPK:OLYMY) and Rexel (OTCPK:RXLSF) on relative weakness and trimmed our holdings in Shell (SHEL) and Evonik (OTCPK:EVKIF)(OTCPK:EVKIY).

The relatively uncertain macro environment and questions over tariffs continue to keep the market on edge, but this volatility can create opportunities for long-term value investors.

data.title: Saturn Oil & Gas: Taking A Victory Lap And Maintaining A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Saturn Oil & Gas (OTCQX:OILSF) (TSX:SOIL:CA) released some "tour de force" quality interim results this week that showcased outperformance in production, the deferral of some budgeted capex to allocate money for "tuck-in" land packages in two of their three core operating areas. The tuck-ins bring 5,400 BOEPD of 65% oil with them at a very competitive price per flowing bbl of $15,900 and 125 net new locations that compete for capital in their Open Hole Multi Lateral-OHML completion strategy.

Saturn has rebounded nicely from early April, when we doubled our position at about $1.00 per share. With the success they are seeing from extending the OHML Pilot at Spearfish, plans are to extend these learnings to the new "tuck-in" acreage in the Midale and Torquay plays.

Obviously, we are pretty pleased with Saturn's performance over the past few months. As it is not a dividend payer, we have no reason to stick around over the short term unless more of the same is on the table. We aren't too invested in the disconnect between the PDP, 1P, or 2P reserve valuations, as the market has heavily discounted those for all upstream companies. That won't change until there is a vibe-shift on the supply side of the oil equation, and that is not likely imminent, although we can see a scenario where it could play out.

Fortunately, we can see a path higher for the company in the coming year and are maintaining a buy near current levels.

The thesis for hanging on to Saturn with a 100% gain

Growth-more BOEPD, exceeding forecasts—cost reduction, some of which comes from growth, future optionality, debt reduction, and, of course, equity concentration, better known as buybacks, are about the only things for which the market is rewarding investors with higher stock prices. And that's not likely to change either. Fortunately, the company is executing on all of those fronts.

Growth/Exceeding Forecasts/Future Optionality: The company is now forecasting YE 2025 exit production of 43-44K BOEPD. That's 12% above guidance posted for the year and driven by drilling prowess - let's understand something: drilling is more than just making a hole in the ground. That's what you end up with, but the journey can be long and hazardous. I have completed Side Track #4 on a couple of wells. Think they ran over-budget? Asset outperformance validates the claims the company has made about its reserves potential, and in this case, it is projected to deliver full-year production of 41-43K BOEPD. The 125 new top-tier drilling locations bring near-term optionality for enhanced deployment of capital.

Cost reduction: One of my early learnings in the oilfield was the universal metric of, "Ok, you showed me you can do it. Now do it cheaper." I cut my teeth in the patch working for Exxon. The cost discipline I learned at the hands of those taskmasters served me well my entire career. I was always the one looking for a way to back out of an operation. It probably accounts for my being continuously employed for all 38 years of my active career. People notice two things in an industrial operation: saving money and improving a process (which in turn saves money.)

During the quarter, management elected to pull back some planned capex on existing properties to take advantage of some tuck-in acreage. This came with production of 5,400 BOEPD and enabled an increase in new drilling locations. As noted above, this pickup is in their most active areas and will be advantaged by OHML completions. As a result, Saturn expects OPEX to decline across the board by 3%, from the $20.60 per BOE forecast to $19.50-20.00 per BOE. More units means a wider base across which to spread costs.

Debt reduction: Saturn's balance sheet isn't all that raggedy. In 2024, they spent $600 mm CAD buying Veren's (now Whitecap Resources (OTCPK:WCPRF)) Flat Lake and Battrum assets to increase their oil weighting. They've paid this down about 15% in the intervening time, from $815 mm CAD to $695 mm CAD, or about $120 mm CAD. That works out to about 1.2X EBITDA and certainly puts their 1X target in sight by YE 2026.

Equity concentration-buybacks: Since August of 2024, through their NCIB and SIB, the company has bought back 11.2 mm shares, or about 5% of the current float. This includes 2 mm for the second quarter. John Jeffrey, CEO discussed the impact of the SIB:

Since Saturn's share price steadily increased following the SIB announcement, including a few days trading above the $2.15 offer price, we've only seen 1.6 million shares tendered out of a total of 7 million shares offered, returning $3.5 million to shareholders. The SIB proved beneficial to all shareholders. Buying back our own shares reflects our view that Saturn's barrels are the highest quality and most undervalued available in the marketplace today.

This is a fairly common claim for oil companies to make, and I have harped on how it rarely seems to be the case. To me, the fact that it has made a difference in Saturn's shares bolsters my view of the current undervaluation of the company.

A catalyst for further price movement in Saturn

I've always said management earns the inflated salaries they pay themselves by creating value. The Viewfield Bakken waterflood falls into that category. Regular readers of my public articles know that I love water and polymer floods. They are the gift of multiple payouts that just keeps on giving and giving over time. I have previously written on this production method here and here.

Saturn's first Viewfield Bakken waterflood project at Creelman has just commenced water injection. The goal is to provide pressure support for new Bakken development wells planned for 2026. 12-18 months from now, the company should start to see some results from this endeavor.

The Creelman waterflood project has the potential to continue to scale up in the future. It is the first stage of a multiyear waterflood program, with the objective of flattening the decline curve and hopefully increasing the ultimate oil recovery. As I've said previously, if the reservoir conditions are right, water floods can increase production-more water equals more oil recovery and an increased reserve estimate. Grant McKenzie, CLO, put some aspirational numbers to the desired upside potential:

To use simple math, if Saturn had a field of 100 million barrels of oil in place and we increased the recovery factor of the field from 6% to 20%, it would represent an incremental 14 million producible barrels. Applying our Q2 '25 netback of $36 per BOE translates into an incremental cash flow of over $500 million.

6% to 20% sounds like value creation to me.

Your takeaway

I think Saturn has earned our trust and is set up for continued growth. The company is trading now at less than 2X NTM EBITDA. That's too cheap for a company delivering the way Saturn has done. I think Saturn goes higher in the coming year on internal performance, capitalizing on the tuck-in acreage. A move back into the upper $60s for WTI would be the icing on the cake.

Year to date, the company is punching well above its weight with price returns of nearly 28%. If we were to give them a multiple of 3X, the shares could easily rerate above $7.00 per share. This is above the top of analyst expectations of $6.00 per share, but even if they are right, Saturn is a triple at current prices.

Wrapping it up. I think Saturn remains a strong buy at current levels, but it's obviously trading well above its 200-day SMA of $1.48 per share. Might we see some consolidation? Perhaps, particularly as EPS estimates are about half for Q-3,4 that we saw in Q-2. As such, we might want to monitor the ticker for a reversion to at least the 50-day SMA of $1.77.

Long term, I think we have a keeper in Saturn, and if oil prices turn up and the margin continues to rise, it wouldn't surprise me to see a cash dividend enter the picture, particularly when the debt target is achieved. Here is John Jeffrey on the subject of M&A and a possible cash payment from the Q&A:

Well, we think our shares are highly undervalued. I think a lot of the response that you're seeing in the market on the other side is also because of our rapid deleveraging. However, in the meantime, we're continuing to pay down debt. But if we do, do an acquisition, does it solve for is our debt materially lower in the next few years than otherwise would be. So these are some of the things we look for before pursuing an acquisition or buybacks or other distributions of cash.

data.title: Dividend Power Dogs: 12 Ideal 'Safer' September Stars
data.description:
Foreword

Only one on this Dividend Power list of 35 was too pricey or revealed skinny dividends! Twelve of the 35 low-priced Dividend Power dogs are ready to buy because they also show “safer” dividends whose free cash-flow yield exceeds dividend yield. September finds Carlyle Secured Lending (CGBD), Stellus Capital (SCM), Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), SLR Investment (SLRC), and Seven Hills Realty (SEVN) as the top five safely living up to the dogcatcher ideal.

About Dividend Power

“How high are the earnings?

This strategy defines a universe of stocks based on earnings yield, with higher earnings yield considered better, because it signifies a low valuation.

How great is the yield?

We sort the universe of stocks by their dividend yield, picking the top 35.

What is the result?

The result is a portfolio that does a relatively good job weathering downturns, while still catching much upward motion during bull markets.

It is one of the simplest strategies that has also proven to be one of the most effective.”—YCharts

See a summary of the top ten break-even priced August Dividend Power Dogs data (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the data for nine IDEAL “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 24.16% To 81.98% Net Gains From Top-Ten DiviPower Dogs By September 2026

Two of the ten top DiviPower stocks (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this August 2025 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 20% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to August 2026 were:

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest-yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called "underdogs".

data.title: Photronics: Growth Ahead At A Deeply Reasonable Price
data.description:
Photronics Inc. (NASDAQ:PLAB) presents a compelling investment opportunity as an undervalued, critical player in the semiconductor supply chain. While the semiconductor industry is currently at the trough of a cyclical downturn, all signs are pointing to a potential recovery in late 2025 or early 2026 thanks to technological advancements in the AI sector and normalizing inventory levels after a long period of oversupply.

Although Photronics is largely exposed to mainstream IC applications, its high-end IC segment has been gaining traction over the past few quarters due to node migration to smaller geometries. This migration is mainly driven by companies focusing on efficiency and shrinking their devices.

The ongoing node migration is also creating opportunities for the company to increase its content in AI applications, which is well-positioned to grow rapidly over the coming years, especially with Nvidia’s 800 V HVDC data center architecture. At the same time, Photronics is well-positioned to gain a stable long-term revenue stream from Tesla’s (TSLA) recent deal with Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) to manufacture its next-generation AI6 chip in Samsung’s Texas fab, considering that Samsung was the company’s second largest customer in FY 2024.

Furthermore, Photronics is less-exposed to the tariff risk, despite depending on China, Taiwan, and South Korea for a majority of its revenue, thanks to its diversified geographical footprint, with manufacturing facilities in each of its main regions. With that in mind, the company is investing $200 million to expand its US manufacturing capacity to capitalize on market opportunities in the US driven by AI and HPC applications. The company also intends to upgrade its Asian facilities to expand its offerings to cater to smaller nodes in its largest markets.

Photronics’ significant investments in its facilities showcase its financial strength. Although the company’s revenues have been declining since last year due to the ongoing semiconductor downturn, it is still a profitable, free-cash-flow-generating business, with a fortress-like balance sheet. In fact, the company holds 42% of its market cap in cash with negligible debt on its balance sheet.

Given the company’s strong fundamentals, a potential semiconductor recovery in the short-term, and its expanding opportunity in high-margin AI and HPC applications, I’m rating Photronics as a buy with a price target of $59 by 2028, implying 153% upside from current levels.

Business Overview

Photronics is a leading global manufacturer of photomasks, which are highly precise quartz or glass plates containing microscopic images of electronic circuits. These photomasks are crucial components in semiconductors’ integrated circuits (ICs) and flat panel displays (FPDs), by transferring circuit patterns onto wafers for ICs and onto FPDs during the fabrication process. Photronics’ business is divided into 2 segments, ICs and FPDs, both of which include high-end photomasks and mainstream photomasks.

For ICs, high-end photomasks serve IC nodes smaller or equal to 28 nanometers, which are suitable for high-growth applications such as HPC, AI and ML, flagship smartphones, and high-end consumer electronics. Meanwhile, mainstream IC photomasks serve IC nodes greater than 28 nanometers which have end markets such as automotive electronics, industrial control systems, IoT devices, and regular consumer electronics.

For FPDs, high-end photomasks include AMOLED, G10.5+, and LTPS photomasks. These photomasks are included in applications such as high-end smartphones, HD and large display TVs, wearable devices, automotive displays, and commercial displays. Meanwhile, mainstream FPD photomasks include G8 and smaller photomasks, which serve end markets such as PC monitors, laptop displays, and mainstream smartphones and tablets.

The IC segment is Photronics’ largest segment, representing 73-74% of its total revenues, while the FPD segment represents around 26-27% of its total revenue. Accordingly, Photronics’ future growth is mainly dependent on the IC segment, especially high-end photomasks since they carry higher ASPs given the level of precision required in the applications they are included in.

With that in mind, Photronics operates in a highly specialized and concentrated market with high entry barriers, mainly due to the capital intensive nature of this business. Currently, the company’s largest competitors are Dai Nippon Printing (OTCPK:DNPCF), Toppan Photomasks (OTCPK:TONPF), and HOYA Corporation (OTCPK:HOCPF), all of which are Japanese companies. At the same time, the company faces competition from smaller local manufacturers in China, making Photronics the leading player in the photomasks industry in the Western world.

Despite this status, Photronics generates the vast majority of its revenues outside the US and Europe, with Taiwan, China, and South Korea being its largest markets, respectively. In Q3 FY 2025, the company generated 77.8% of its revenues from outside the Western world, up from 77% in the prior year. The company also generated 79.4% of its YTD revenues from outside the western world compared to 79.2% in the same prior year period.

Given this reliance on Asian markets for a significant chunk of its revenues, Photronics has an impressive global manufacturing footprint. The company has 11 manufacturing facilities strategically located in its largest markets to offer localized service and short lead times to its customers. These facilities are located in the US, Europe, China, Taiwan, and South Korea.

Photronics’ emphasis on localized services is a major tailwind to its business, especially with the ongoing tariff uncertainty, as it minimizes imports to tariffed countries. This significantly reduces Photronics’ exposure to tariff-related duties and supply chain disruptions.

Financial Overview

Top-Line Analysis

Given that ICs represent the largest revenue stream for Photronics, the company is impacted by the general conditions of the semiconductor industry since IC sales are dependent on new wafer buildouts. As such, Photronics’ revenues have been declining since FY 2024, in line with the broader industry.

In FY 2024, the company reported a 2.8% YoY revenue decline from $892 million to $866.9 million, driven by a 2% decline in IC revenues from $651.3 million to $638.2 million and a 5% decline in FPD revenues from $240.8 million to $228.8 million. The revenue slowdown has caused Photronics’ gross margin to contract YoY from 37.7% to 36.4% in FY 2024.

However, a deeper look shows that high-end ICs saw 17% YoY growth during that period from $194.9 million to $228.5 million to represent 35.8% of IC revenues compared to 29.9% in FY 2023.

The company has previously highlighted this opportunity in its FQ 2 2025 earnings call, stating that it is seeing strong foundry demand for 22 and 28 nanometer photomask products in Asia.

This opportunity could be a major growth tailwind in the coming years, as I will discuss later in this report, especially with high-end ICs carrying a higher ASP profile compared to the rest of Photronics’ product line-up.

Bottom-Line Analysis

In terms of profitability, Photronics remains a profitable business, albeit impacted by the shrinking revenues caused by the semiconductor industry’s slowdown. In FY 2024, the company reported an EBIT of $221.6 million compared to $253 million in the prior year, at an EBIT margin of 25.6% compared to 28.4% in FY 2023.

This was mainly caused by the revenue slowdown as well as higher operating costs, highlighted by a 21.4% increase in R&D spending.

As the company is now expected to report around $80 million in CapEx in FQ4 2025, it is likely to report negative free cash flow during the upcoming quarter. This trend could continue into FY 2026 since the company announced plans to upgrade its Asian facilities to support the production of smaller node photomasks as well as replacing its equipment that reached their end-of-life. According to the Q3 earnings call, this CapEx cycle is expected to last 3 years, which could see its FCF margins under pressure during that period.

Despite the short-term disruption to its free cash flow generation, Photronics’ expansion plan comes at an opportune time to capitalize on the reshoring of semiconductor manufacturing in the US, as part of the Trump administration’s “America First” policy. Similarly, the company’s investments in upgrading its Asian facilities allows it to increase its AI-related business in Asia. Accordingly, these investments could see Photronics’ margins expand in the long-term as its high-end IC photomask sales increase.

Semiconductor Recovery

As mentioned earlier, Photronics is impacted by the cyclicality of the semiconductor industry since photomasks are an enabler technology directly tied to the production and design cycles of chips. In fact, photomasks are fundamental for wafer fabrication since every new chip design and every significant upgrade to an existing chip design requires a new set of photomasks. Accordingly, when demand for electronic devices increases, and chip manufacturers ramp up their production, their need for photomasks increases, which is the main tailwind to Photronics’ business.

In prior coverages on Aehr Test Systems (AEHR) and Axcelis Technologies (ACLS), I discussed the potential for a cyclical semiconductor recovery later this year or early 2026. First of all, Photronics’ mainstream segment stands to be a major beneficiary from a potential rebound in consumer electronics.

This is directly tied to Deutsche Bank Research forecasting 6%, 5%, and 4% growth in computing, consumer electronics, and automotive sectors, respectively, in 2025. In this way, Photronics is likely to see a rebound in “mainstream” photomasks revenues in Q1 FY 2026, since its fiscal year ends in October of any given CY. This forecast is supported by data from McKinsey showing that inventories are declining to more normalized levels, in line with pre-pandemic years.

Moreover, early offshoots of this potential recovery were seen in Q1 wafer shipment data from SEMI, showing that total silicon wafer shipments increased by 2.2% YoY during the quarter. This trend can also be seen in SEMI’s Q2 wafer shipment data which showed a 9.6% YoY increase in global silicon wafer shipments. Q2 shipments were also 14.9% higher than the first quarter, indicating signs of recovery in sectors beyond AI, which further supports my conviction of a broader industry rebound in late 2025/early 2026.

Furthermore, the SEMI World Fab Forecast Report showed that a wave of investments in wafer fabrication capacity expansion is underway, with 32 fabs launching this year and construction on 18 new fabs is expected to start this year, the majority of which will be online in 2026 and 2027. These growing investments, mainly driven by AI-fueled demand, are especially a promising sign for Photronics since they create demand for its most advanced high-end IC photomasks, which carry higher ASPs and gross margins.

AI Opportunity

On that note, the expected recovery in high-end applications is mainly driven by the growth of the AI ecosystem, positioning Photronics to capture a significant opportunity in this sector thanks to its fundamental role in the fabrication of advanced chips.

These AI applications, in areas such as data centers, HPC, and edge AI, demand complex, smaller, faster, and more power-efficient chips, which usually come in nodes equal or smaller than 7 nanometers. This expected growth in the AI ecosystem will also be supported by Nvidia’s next-generation 800 V HVDC data center architecture, which has higher semiconductor content compared to current data center architectures.

Given the small size of these advanced chips, they require highly precise photomasks, which is in line with Photronics expertise. As chipmakers continue to introduce more advanced chip designs to push AI advancements to the edge, they require more photomasks per design.

At the same time, as these designs become more intricate, they also lead to a higher ASP per photomask set due to the level of complexity of these chips. This combination of higher ASPs and more photomask content per chip design could be long-term tailwinds to Photronics’ revenues and profitability.

One such opportunity Photronics has is Samsung’s latest $16.5 billion deal with Tesla to manufacture the latter’s AI6 chip in Samsung’s Taylor, Texas fab. The deal is a multi-year agreement extending through 2033, and Elon Musk stated that the $16.5 billion figure is “just the bare minimum”. While no timeline was provided for AI6 chip production, analysts expect production to commence in 2027-2028, as Tesla’s AI5 chips are set to be produced at the end of 2026, as confirmed by Musk in Tesla’s latest earnings call.

The AI6 chip is expected to be the foundation of Tesla’s future EVs and the core of its self-driving vehicles and Optimus humanoid robots. This could be a sign of a significant period of production, driving demand for photomasks and positioning Photronics to capture a significant market opportunity.

The reason Photronics is likely to be a winner from this deal is that Samsung was its second largest customer in FY 2024, as the company’s latest 10-K filing stated the following (emphasis added):

During 2024, we sold our products to approximately 675 customers. Revenue from United Microelectronics Corp. Co., Ltd. accounted for approximately 15%, 14% and 15% of our total revenues in 2024, 2023 and 2022, respectively, and revenue from Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. accounted for approximately 12%, 10% and 11% of our total revenues in those respective years. In addition, revenue from Semiconductor Manufacturing International Corporation accounted for approximately 9%, 13% and 5% of our total revenues in 2024, 2023 and 2022, respectively

In this way, the deal could provide Photronics with a stable, long-term, high-margin revenue stream given that the AI6 chip will be in 4 and 3 nanometer nodes, with the potential to further shrink to 2 nanometer nodes in the future.

Node Migration

Beyond this opportunity in core processors, Photronics has other opportunities in supporting the AI ecosystem in advanced packaging and interconnects. With ever-increasing AI workloads, the need for fast interconnects, including silicon photonics and advanced packaging solutions, is also increasing. This is driving demand for photomasks since they are crucial for creating the patterns required for these technologies, which allow multiple dies to be stacked and interconnected to improve the performance of AI systems.

The increasing complexity and demand for advanced chips are directly leading to a phenomenon that is set to be a major growth driver for Photronics over the coming years, node migration. The node migration in the IC market is driven by the continuous shrinking of transistor sizes and the advancement to newer and smaller process technologies to benefit from the performance and efficiency gains offered by advanced nodes.

Currently, the IC market is in the midst of a significant node migration that is expected to continue and even accelerate in the coming years due to demand for advanced nodes in the rapidly growing AI and HPC sectors. At the same time, node migration is also driven by the EV and autonomous driving sectors that require high-performance chips for battery management and ADAS.

With chip designs continuously shrinking and becoming more complex, they require more mask layers per chip and higher precision. This in turn creates a significant market opportunity for Photronics thanks to its strength in high-end IC photomasks.

To that end, Photronics is planning capability extensions to its Asian facilities to allow for the production of 8-nanometer and 6-nanometer photomasks, with a target of realizing revenues from this endeavor by late FY 2027 or early FY 2028. By extending its offerings to cater to smaller nodes, the company could gain significant content from customers developing high-end processors for end-markets such as memory, EV, and communications.

With that in mind, Photronics is already capable of producing 8 and 6-nanometer photomasks in its Idaho facility in the US, being the only US company able to achieve this feat onshore, as mentioned in the Q3 earnings call. This provides it with a significant edge with US hyperscalers developing their own AI chips.

Revenue Projections

To forecast Photronics' revenues through 2028, I considered the impact of the current cyclical trough in the semiconductor industry, a potential recovery in late CY 2025, rapid growth in the AI and HPC sectors, a moderate growth in EVs and autonomous vehicles, and Tesla’s deal with Samsung. Based on these factors, my revenue targets for Photronics are as follows.

For FY 2025, I’m forecasting a 3.3% YoY revenue decline to $838.5 million, reflecting the ongoing semiconductor downturn, especially given Photronics fiscal year ends in October, the first month of CY Q4. As such, the company has limited potential to benefit from a broader recovery in late CY 2025. This forecast is also consistent with Photronics’ FQ4 2025 revenue guidance between $201 to $209 million.

For FY 2026, I’m forecasting 5% YoY revenue growth to $880.4 million, expecting that Photronics fully captures the early stages of the broader semiconductor industry recovery. I expect this growth to be driven by demand for high-end IC photomasks and a rebound in mainstream photomasks as consumer electronics and automotive sectors improve.

For FY 2027, I’m forecasting 6% YoY revenue growth, accelerating from FY 2026, to $933.3 million as node migration intensifies due to demand for high-performance chips in EVs and autonomous driving. This forecast also takes into consideration initial revenue contributions from the Samsung-Tesla deal for the production of AI6 chips.

For FY 2028, I’m forecasting 8% YoY revenue growth to $1 billion. This accelerated growth reflects the full impact of rapid node migration in AI and HPC applications, supported by Photronics’ finalized capability extensions in Asian facilities, driven by widespread adoption of Nvidia’s 800 V HVDC data center architecture. This forecast also reflects higher revenues from the Samsung-Tesla deal as AI6 chip production ramps up.

Valuation

Given this revenue forecast, I will be forecasting Photronics’ EBIT through FY 2028 to reach my price target for the stock as I will be taking an EV/EBIT approach. The reasons behind this methodology are that Photronics is already a mature, profitable, free-cash-flow-generating company, while also taking the high depreciation costs associated with its business into consideration.

Accordingly, I’m forecasting Photronics’ EBIT and EBIT margins through FY 2028 as follows.

For FY 2025, I’m forecasting an EBIT of $199.1 million reflecting a 23.7% EBIT margin, anticipating EBIT margin compression from 25.6% reported in FY 2024. This forecast is consistent with management’s FQ4 EBIT margin guidance between 20-22%. That said, it should be noted that Photronics has a history of beating its own guidance, as witnessed by its FQ3 earnings results compared to the guidance issued in FQ2.

For FY 2026, I’m forecasting an EBIT of $224.5 million, reflecting YoY EBIT margin expansion to 25.5%. This forecast is driven by the potential semiconductor industry recovery, combined with improved operating leverage and a growing contribution from higher gross margin high-end IC segment.

For FY 2027, I’m forecasting continued EBIT margin expansion to 27.5%, leading to an EBIT of $256.6 million. This forecast is supported by accelerating revenue growth from node migration and the initial ramp-up of high-margin AI opportunities, including the Samsung-Tesla deal, further improving operating leverage.

For FY 2028, I’m forecasting $292.3 million in EBIT, reflecting a 29% EBIT margin. This forecast is driven by robust revenue growth and a favorable mix towards high-end IC photomasks, which command higher ASPs and gross margins. The EBIT margin forecast is also slightly higher than the 28.4% EBIT margin reported in FY 2023, where Photronics reported all-time-record revenues, showcasing improved profitability from advanced technologies.

At its current share price of $23.41, Photronics has a market cap of $1.38 billion, which translates to an EV of $805.5 million after considering its net cash position of $575.8 million. As such, the stock is trading at an EV/EBIT multiple of 4.04 relative to my FY 2025 EBIT forecast.

Photronics’ current valuation represents a discount to its historical EV/EBIT multiples, as its 3-year median stands at 5.042, 5-year median stands at 6.007, and 7-year median stands at 8.183. The gap between Photronics’ current EV/EBIT multiple and its historical levels is primarily due to the broader semiconductor industry downturn.

However, the industry appears to be heading back to growth in late 2025 or early 2026 which can drive a rerating in the coming quarters. With that in mind, the YChart below shows that Photronics traded at an EV/EBIT multiple around 14 during the previous industry peak in 2020-2021.

In this way, the upcoming cycle could see the stock return to trade at a similar EV/EBIT multiple, considering Photronics’ high-growth catalysts related to the Tesla-Samsung deal, node migration, and the full realization of its capability extensions in Asia. All of these factors are major tailwinds to the company’s high-margin high-end IC segment due to the level of specialization required for edge AI chips.

At the same time, Photronics’ largest competitors in the photomask market, Dai Nippon, Toppan, and HOYA, are trading at EV/EBIT multiples of 11.95, 14.30, and 25.17, respectively.

Despite this, my target EV/EBIT multiple for Photronics is 10, a discount compared to its largest competitors since they all have diversified businesses, offering products far beyond photomasks. At the same time, my target multiple is consistent with Photronics’ historical multiple during growth cycles in the semiconductor industry.

Based on this target multiple, my long-term price target for Photronics is $59.30, representing a potential upside of 153%.

Risks

The first risk to consider before investing in Photronics is cyclicality of the semiconductor industry. While signs are pointing to a recovery in late CY 2025 or early CY 2026, a prolonged inventory correction, or slower recovery in consumer electronics and automotive sectors, could lead to slower growth compared to my forecast. This could have an impact on Photronics’ profitability and in turn its valuation, even at the stock’s current muted valuation metrics.

Moreover, a slower recovery could delay transitions to smaller nodes, potentially leading to a slower-than-expected ramp-up in AI and HPC applications. In this way, the forecasted demand for Photronics’ high-end photomasks may not materialize as expected, impacting both revenue growth and EBIT margin expansion.

Another main risk to consider is related to Photronics’ and Samsung’s execution. With Photronics investing $200 million to expand its US manufacturing capabilities, project delays, cost overruns, and yield challenges could be a drag on Photronics’ profitability and cash flow generation, and could also impact its opportunities that were the main driver for this expansion.

This is also relevant with Samsung following its deal with Tesla. If the production ramp for the AI6 chip is delayed beyond the expected 2027-2028 timeline or if Samsung has yield issues in the Taylor fab, the anticipated revenues and margin benefits from this deal to Photronics may not fully materialize or be delayed. This would be a major headwind to the bullish thesis on Photronics.

Finally, the majority of Photronics’ revenues are concentrated within a limited customer base, despite selling to 675 customers in FY 2024. As is, the company’s top 5 customers accounted for 50%, 51%, and 45% of its total revenues in FY 2024, 2023, and 2022, respectively. This exposes Photronics to lose a significant portion of its revenues if any of these customers reduces their photomasks orders or even moves their photomasks production in-house. This could have a devastating impact on Photronics’ financials.

Conclusion

In summary, Photronics presents an interesting investment opportunity at its current valuation. Despite maintaining its profitability and cash flow generation during an industry-wide downturn, its stock is trading at a significant discount to other photomask manufacturers. With a semiconductor industry recovery expected in late CY 2025 to early CY 2026, Photronics has a significant opportunity to return to growth in FY 2026, especially with the momentum its high-end IC photomasks are seeing during the current cyclical trough.

Furthermore, the ongoing node migration in the IC market driven by technology advancements in the AI and HPC sectors, could be a long-term growth tailwind to Photronics. As is, the company likely has direct exposure to the $16.5 billion Samsung-Tesla deal given that Samsung was its second largest customer in FY 2024.

Given these tailwinds, I’m forecasting Photronics to grow its top-line at a 6.3% CAGR from FY 2025 to FY 2028, mainly driven by its high-end IC segment. This growth opportunity could be a major tailwind to Photronics’ gross margins and EBIT margins, potentially supporting a rerating as the semiconductor industry’s next growth phase approaches. In light of these factors, and the stock’s steep discount compared to historical trends and industry peers, I’m rating Photronics as a buy with a price target of $59 by 2028, representing 153% upside from its current valuation.
