data.title: 2 High-Yield, Investment-Grade Bonds For Your Retirement Portfolio
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities.

The stock market is an institution filled with drama. However, unlike the theater that we enjoy for entertainment, this drama has money at stake, leading to heightened emotions among participants. Financial turmoil can arise in mysterious and unpredictable ways; vulnerabilities range from operational shifts to market risks. Economic events can act as the catalyst to fuel volatility in your financial well-being.

Every investment carries risks, but the level of exposure varies across the company’s capital structure. By investing higher up in the capital structure, you can immunize your investment from most risks while keeping a steady stream of income pouring cash into your account. Securities that carry investment-grade ratings are perceived to come with a certain degree of safety due to strong operating fundamentals, financial position, and outlook, as determined by independent agencies. The investment-grade status indicates a lower risk of default and a stronger ability to meet financial obligations and commitments.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we maintain a diversified portfolio of over 50 individual preferred securities and baby bonds. Fixed income represents a significant portion of our investment mix, providing much-needed immunity from most market drama. This allows us to focus on a steady income rather than the market drama. Let us now discuss our top investment-grade fixed income picks.

Pick #1: Athene Preferreds and Baby Bond—Up to 7.4% Yields

U.S. annuity sales remain strong, totaling $105.4 billion in the first quarter of 2025. This marks the sixth consecutive quarter with annuity sales topping $100 billion. Athene is a leading retirement services company with over $400 billion of total assets as of June 30, 2025, and operations in the United States, Bermuda, Canada, and Japan. The company is consistently ranked #1 in U.S. annuity sales and is a wholly owned subsidiary of Apollo Global Management (APO). Yet, Athene operates as a separate credit group with its own capital structure and carries A1 / A+ / A+ /A+ ratings by Moody’s, S&P, Fitch, and AM Best, respectively.

Preferred Securities

  • 6.35% Fixed-to-Float, Non-Cumulative, Series A, Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (ATH.PR.A) – Yield 6.3%

  • 5.625% Fixed Rate, Non-Cumulative, Series B, Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (ATH.PR.B) – Yield 6.3%

  • 4.875% Fixed Rate, Non-Cumulative, Series D, Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (ATH.PR.D) – Yield 6.4%

  • 7.750% Rate Reset, Non-Cumulative, Series E, Redeemable Perpetual Preferred (ATH.PR.E) – Yield 7.4%.

Athene ended Q2 with $10.3 billion in cash and cash equivalents and reported $1.4 billion in net income during 1H 2025. This compares to $90 million in preferred dividends paid during the first six months of its fiscal year. In May, Athene redeemed its Series C preferred when it became callable, since this was a rate-reset type that carried a high interest rate of 5Y T-bill + 5.97% per annum, resetting every five years.

At this time, ATH-D presents an excellent fixed-rate bargain at a deep discount, with 6.% QDI and ~32% upside to par. Upcoming rate cuts would serve as a strong tailwind for its market price, making it a good investment now while it is still on sale. However, given its lower coupon, this preferred is unlikely to be redeemed in the near future.

Baby Bond

  • 7.25% Rate-Reset Junior Subordinated Debentures due 3/30/2064 (ATHS) – Yield 7.0%.

Athene also has a baby bond that trades slightly above par, currently offering a 7% yield. This bond is callable in March 2029 (and will likely be redeemed), presenting 3.5 years of steady interest payments.

Pick#2: AFG Baby Bonds—Up to 6.4% Yield

American Financial Group, Inc. (AFG) is a leader in the specialty property and casualty marketplace, with a history tracing back to 1959. The company’s Specialty P&C Group comprises over 35 diversified businesses and operates in three broad segments. Source.

AFG has consistently made it on Ward’s 50 P&C Top Performers and has been rated A (Excellent) or better by AM Best for over 115 years, making it one of only three insurers to carry such stellar industry credentials. For more than 12 consecutive years, AFG has maintained a Combined Ratio below 94%, underscoring the company’s consistent underwriting strength. Combined Ratio is an insurance metric that indicates the profitability of the core business. A high-level calculation would be (Claims Paid + Operating Expenses) / Premiums Collected. If the ratio is below 100%, it means that the company is making an underwriting profit. A ratio above 100% implies the company is paying out more than it collects.

AFG maintains a $16 billion investment portfolio, with 95% of its fixed maturities carrying Investment-Grade ratings.

AFG has delivered substantially better results than the S&P 500 (SP500) over the past 20 years, with dividends being at the forefront of the company’s returns to shareholders.

The company recently delivered a 10% YoY increase to its annual dividend, marking 20 years of consecutive annual dividend growth. The 10-Yr CAGR in AFG’s annual dividend is an impressive 12.3%. AFG’s management has significant skin in the game, with 24% of common shares owned by the founding family, executives, and the company’s retirement plan. This indicates a strong alignment of interests between insiders and shareholders.

During the first six months of FY 2025, AFG generated $3.7 billion in revenues, with $328 million in net earnings. The company spent $38 million on interest expenses during this period and reported $1.2 billion in cash and cash equivalents on its balance sheet.

  • 5.875% Subordinated Debentures due March 30, 2059 (AFGB) – Yield 6.4%

  • 5.125% Subordinated Debentures due December 15, 2059 (AFGC) – Yield 6.2%

  • 5.625% Subordinated Debentures due June 1, 2060 (AFGD) – Yield 6.4%

  • 4.50% Subordinated Debentures due September 15, 2060 (AFGE) – Yield 5.9%.

AFG has four investment-grade BBB+ rated baby bonds, offering up to 6.4% yields, trading at deep discounts to par. These are long-duration bonds, maturing only in 2059-60, implying a higher level of sensitivity to interest rate changes. As of today, all four baby bonds are callable, but it is unlikely that it would happen.

Currently, AFGE in particular offers a 5.9% yield and ~31% upside to par. AFGC yields 6.2% and presents a ~22% upside to par. With relatively lower coupons and higher upside to par, these present attractive investments to collect steady income for several years.

Conclusion

To successfully navigate the financial markets, investors must have a clear understanding of market risks and need to operate with discipline. To be a successful investor, you must operate unemotionally. But humans are emotional beings, making it far easier to preach than practice.

No investment is completely immune to market risks, but high-quality preferreds and baby bonds have the ability to shield the investor from most. Steady income and better immunity to market noise from these securities make them investments that you don’t have to babysit.

At High Dividend Opportunities, our focus on maintaining a diversified fixed-income portfolio is to bring stability and peace of mind through unpredictable markets. This is what you need for a retirement you deserve: a stress-free retirement.

data.title: Roivant: Brepocitinib's DM Data Impress, Pipeline Focus Narrows
data.description:
Roivant Sciences Overview

Roivant Sciences (NASDAQ:ROIV) stock appears to be finally breaking out of a multi-year trading range. The catalyst? Positive Phase 3 data evaluating brepocitinib (“brep”) for the treatment of dermatomyositis (DM).

My last eval of ROIV came in February and previewed its Q4 2024 earnings report, as well as discussed two of their key assets (brep and mosliciguat). Although in previous articles, I had been bullish on ROIV, my patience was admittedly wearing thin.

I did point out brep Phase 2 data in non-infectious uveitis. Roivant’s subsidiary, Priovant, is sponsoring the Phase 3 trial in this indication (CLARITY). So, is it possible that Roivant has another “pipeline in a product” with brep?

The article that follows reviews the latest data and models brep's market potential in DM. Given the stock's recent appreciation, the risk/reward profile in ROIV appears unchanged, meriting an unchanged Hold rating.

Recent Developments

On its website, Priovant describes brep as an “oral once-daily dual inhibitor of TYK2 and JAK1.”

In the Phase 3 (VALOR) study in DM, 30 mg each day achieved a mean Total Improvement Score, or TIS, of 46.5 versus 31.2 for placebo at 52 weeks (p=0.0006). Moreover, all secondary endpoints, such as skin, muscle, and steroid-sparing, also hit. With JAK inhibitors, people are always wary of thromboembolic risk (blood clots). However, for brep, safety was “consistent with prior trials,” with no higher rates of malignancy or MACE (heart attack, stroke) versus placebo. The company intends to submit a U.S. marketing application in 1H26.

The only approved treatment in DM is IVIG/Octagam 10%. However, monthly IVIG is burdensome and expensive. Steroids and immunosuppressants, like methotrexate, are also utilized in DM, but come with many side effects (especially with long-term use) and have mixed efficacy.

VALOR data signals that brep is a differentiated option in DM. An oral, daily option with a safety profile that avoids class issues and allows for the tapering of steroids is a highly attractive proposition.

As for the deal mechanics surrounding Pfizer (PFE) and Roivant in Priovant. Pfizer holds 25% of Priovant, while Roivant controls 74%. Priovant originally licensed brep from Pfizer and controls U.S. and Japan rights. Priovant is pegged for sub-teens royalties to Pfizer on U.S./Japan sales.

It has been estimated that there are around 31,000 U.S. persons with DM. For the following drug DCF calculator, I assumed that half of them will be eligible for systemic treatment. I used a peak share of 33% if efficacy/label is strong and payers prefer oral over IVIG burden or off-label treatment. Small-molecule immunology tends to be ~$80k WAC (e.g., RINVOQ, SOTYKTU), but I went with $70k to account for rebates.

So, I have peak annual U.S. sales of $543M. A rule of thumb is that ROW sales are 1x U.S. sales. While there are more people ROW, net prices are typically lower and access lags. In any event, we are looking at a potential blockbuster in DM. In fact, I think this puppy is worth at least $3 billion at this moment, with Roivant’s share being $2.2 billion. Given Roivant’s history (it recently exited two commercial-stage assets at strong terms), it may be interested in selling its share. In any event, it gives it a notable commercial asset, which is nice to have if you’re running a pharma company, right?

Outside of Priovant, Immunovant (IMVT) continues to advance batoclimab in Graves’ disease, as well as 1402 in a variety of autoimmune conditions. I have written on Immunovant in the past (see article). 1402 is Immunovant’s “economic engine” so long as safety/LDL profile stays clean. It will also have to deal with some competition in that space. Meanwhile, Pulmovant’s mosliciguat remains in a Phase 2 study for pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease. I briefly touched on mosliciguat's prospects in PH-ILD in my last article on ROIV (click here).

The Bottom Line

To wrap up, brep’s win provides Roivant with another savvy commercial-stage (NDA preparations) drug. Moving forward, Roivant is concentrated on brep (near-term) and 1402 (medium-term) with $4.5 billion in cash and ST investments to bridge both. So, execution in the next 18-24 months is mainly 1) brep filing/label/access, 2) 1402 enrollment pace + first late-stage readouts, (3) capital deployment (BD vs more buybacks).

If one thing is for certain, it is that execution is paramount for Roivant. I mentioned the enormous cash pile, but the company is “burning” (cash from operations) ~$200 million every quarter. Moreover, the company is graciously buying back its own shares – having just completed a $1.5 billion program.

From my porch, I’m still at odds with Roivant’s “vant” model (which is somewhat dependent on replicating “Telavant-like success”), and the market’s ~7% price appreciation of ROIV post-VALOR appears to account for brep's increased probability of success (55% → 90%). So, I’d like to hold steady on ROIV (maintain Hold)
